* 所在地 - 日高町原谷362
=== 内ノ畑王子 === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|内ノ畑王子跡]] 馬留王子から[[和歌山県道176号井関御坊線]]沿いに南に進み、西川を渡ったところにある今熊野神社の前の川岸が'''内ノ畑王子'''（うちのはたおうじ）の旧址である（日高町萩原1500<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89">長谷川[2007: 89]</ref>）。
『中右記』には見えず、「熊野道間之愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に「内ノハタ王子」の名を記すほか、木の枝を刈って槌を作り、[[サカキ|榊]]の枝を結びつけて王子に持参する風習があったとしており、同様の記述が『熊野詣日記』（[[応永]]34年〈[[1427年]]〉）にも見られる<ref name="Heibon_1997_109">平凡社[1997: 109]</ref>。こうした風習により、当地では槌王子（つちおうじ）とも呼ばれた<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89"/>。
日高町には槌王子という字がある（日高町萩原1507<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89"/>）が現在の社地とは離れていることから、もともと字槌王子にあった王子が近世までに衰退・荒廃し、現在の社地に移祀されたと推定されており、『紀伊続風土記』にもこの推定を裏付ける記述がある<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/>。[[1908年]]（明治41年）、原谷の今熊野神社に合祀され、その後内原王子神社に合祀された。今熊野王子社隣地の内ノ畑王子跡は日高町指定史跡（1973年〈昭和48年〉6月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
*所在地 - 日高町萩原1500
=== 高家王子 === {{神社 |名称 = 内原王子神社 |画像 =[[File:高家王子.jpg|200px]] |所在地 = 和歌山県日高郡日高町萩原1670 |位置 = |祭神 = 皇太神 |別名 = 高家王子、東光寺王子 |例祭 = 10月16日・17日 }} 内ノ畑王子から西川沿いに南へ進み、内原王子神社の社地が'''高家王子'''（たいえおうじ）の旧址である。
『中右記』天仁2年（1109年）10月19日条に「大家王子」の名が見えるが、「熊野道間之愚記」（建仁元年〈1201年〉）には地名は見えるものの王子の記述はなく、『[[源平盛衰記]]』巻四〇に[[平維盛]]が参詣したとある<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/>。王子のある字王子脇に隣接して東光寺という地名が残ることから、近世には「若一王子社」「東光寺王子社」の名でも呼ばれ（『続紀伊風土記』）、法華堂・祈願堂・不動堂・毘沙門堂・護摩堂といった別当寺院を従えていた<ref>平凡社[1997: 109-110]</ref>。
高家の名の由来は、当王子が、中世の高家荘5ケ村の総氏神であったことによると考えられている<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/><ref>西[1987: 122]</ref>。異説によれば、現比定地の北が旧址であるといい、その根拠として『中右記』にある「高家荘司の仮屋」と現比定地との位置関係に矛盾があること、また現比定地の旧地名が王子脇であることが挙げられている<ref name="Heibon_1997_110">平凡社[1997: 110]</ref>。
例年10月16日・17日（[[1897年]]〈明治30年〉までは[[旧暦]]9月）に、近隣の萩原・荊木・池田・高家の4地域が集まって例祭「萩原祭」が執行される<ref name="Heibon_1997_110"/>。高家王子跡は和歌山県県指定史跡（1958年〈昭和33年〉4月1日指定）<ref>{{Cite web |publisher=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-10-04 }}</ref>。
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 小山 靖憲、2000、『熊野古道』、岩波書店（岩波新書） ISBN 4004306655 * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * — 、1983、『和歌山県の地名』、平凡社（日本歴史地名大系31）
== 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Kujuku-ojis|九十九王子}} * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (広川町)#馬留王子|馬留王子]]） - '''沓掛王子''' - '''馬留王子''' - '''内ノ畑王子''' - '''高家王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (御坊市)#善童子王子|善童子王子]]） * [[鹿ヶ瀬峠]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html 文化財・史跡明細] - 日高町教育委員会サイト内 {{九十九王子}} {{神道 横}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうしひたかちよう}} [[Category:九十九王子|ひたかちよう]] [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県内の市町村指定史跡]] [[Category:和歌山県日高町の歴史]]

本項目では、[[和歌山県]][[日高郡 (和歌山県)|日高郡]][[日高町 (和歌山県)|日高町]]に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から [[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。
本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が和歌山県日高郡日高町にある王子を扱う。
== 日高町の九十九王子 == 日高町の九十九王子は4社。
=== 沓掛王子 === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|沓掛王子跡]] [[九十九王子 (広川町)#馬留王子|馬留王子]]（和歌山県[[有田郡]][[広川町 (和歌山県)|広川町]]）から南西に進み、急な坂道の[[鹿ヶ瀬峠]]（ししがせとうげ）の古道を越え、峠道を下りきったあたりを古い地名で王子谷といい、「熊野道間之愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「超此山参沓カケ王子」と記された'''沓掛王子'''（くつかけおうじ）の旧址である<ref name="Heibon_1997_234">平凡社[1997: 234]</ref>（日高町原谷902<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86">長谷川[2007: 86]</ref>）。
後世には鍵掛王子（かぎかけおうじ）と呼ばれたと見え、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』に「鍵掛王子 山口にあり、境内に弁財天社・長床あり」と記されていることを根拠に、前述の旧址とは別の字被喜の弁財天社（日高町原谷845<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86"/>）社地が社址と見なされてきた。しかし、「熊野道間之愚記」の記述と矛盾することや、王子谷に古くから小祠があって祭祀も行われていたことなどから<ref name="Heibon_1997_234"/>、時期は不明ながら弁財天社に移祀されたものと推定されている<ref name="Heibon_1997_234"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86"/>。
[[1877年]]（[[明治]]10年）、弁財天社とともに原谷皇太神社に合祀された<ref name="Heibon_1997_234"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86"/>。字被喜の沓掛王子跡は日高町指定史跡（1973年〈昭和48年〉6月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web |publisher=日高町教育委員会 |url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html |title=文化財・史跡明細 |accessdate=2009-10-04 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 - 日高町原谷845
=== 馬留王子 === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|馬留王子跡]] 沓掛王子から再び南西に進み、原谷皇太神社を過ぎて光明寺の境内に隣り合って'''馬留王子'''（うまどめおうじ）の石碑が立っている。しかし、『[[和歌山県聖蹟]]』は、実際の跡は石碑より上のミカン畑の中であるとしている<ref>西[1987: 120]</ref>。
『[[中右記]]』[[天仁]]2年（[[1109年]]）10月18日条に「馬留」の地名は見えるものの王子については触れられておらず、「熊野道間之愚記」（建仁元年〈1201年〉）にも記述は見えず、『紀伊続風土記』は「馬留王子社」について境内60[[間]]と記している<ref>平凡社[1997: 115]</ref>。現地では「間（ハザマ）王子」「駒留王子神社」とも称し、有田郡広川町の[[九十九王子 (広川町)#馬留王子|馬留王子]]と区別するために、広川町の馬留王子を東ノ馬留王子、日高町の馬留王子を西ノ馬留王子と称することがある<ref>長谷川[2007: 85-87]</ref>。
[[1907年]]（明治40年）、原谷皇太神社に合祀された<ref>長谷川[2007: 87]</ref>。日高町指定史跡（1973年〈昭和48年〉6月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 - 日高町原谷362
=== 内ノ畑王子 === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|内ノ畑王子跡]] 馬留王子から[[和歌山県道176号井関御坊線]]沿いに南に進み、西川を渡ったところにある今熊野神社の前の川岸が'''内ノ畑王子'''（うちのはたおうじ）の旧址である（日高町萩原1500<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89">長谷川[2007: 89]</ref>）。
『中右記』には見えず、「熊野道間之愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に「内ノハタ王子」の名を記すほか、木の枝を刈って槌を作り、[[サカキ|榊]]の枝を結びつけて王子に持参する風習があったとしており、同様の記述が『熊野詣日記』（[[応永]]34年〈[[1427年]]〉）にも見られる<ref name="Heibon_1997_109">平凡社[1997: 109]</ref>。こうした風習により、当地では槌王子（つちおうじ）とも呼ばれた<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89"/>。
日高町には槌王子という字がある（日高町萩原1507<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89"/>）が現在の社地とは離れていることから、もともと字槌王子にあった王子が近世までに衰退・荒廃し、現在の社地に移祀されたと推定されており、『紀伊続風土記』にもこの推定を裏付ける記述がある<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/>。[[1908年]]（明治41年）、原谷の今熊野神社に合祀され、その後内原王子神社に合祀された。今熊野王子社隣地の内ノ畑王子跡は日高町指定史跡（1973年〈昭和48年〉6月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
*所在地 - 日高町萩原1500
=== 高家王子 === {{神社 |名称 = 内原王子神社 |画像 =[[File:高家王子.jpg|200px]] |所在地 = 和歌山県日高郡日高町萩原1670 |位置 = |祭神 = 皇太神 |別名 = 高家王子、東光寺王子 |例祭 = 10月16日・17日 }} 内ノ畑王子から西川沿いに南へ進み、内原王子神社の社地が'''高家王子'''（たいえおうじ）の旧址である。
『中右記』天仁2年（1109年）10月19日条に「大家王子」の名が見えるが、「熊野道間之愚記」（建仁元年〈1201年〉）には地名は見えるものの王子の記述はなく、『[[源平盛衰記]]』巻四〇に[[平維盛]]が参詣したとある<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/>。王子のある字王子脇に隣接して東光寺という地名が残ることから、近世には「若一王子社」「東光寺王子社」の名でも呼ばれ（『続紀伊風土記』）、法華堂・祈願堂・不動堂・毘沙門堂・護摩堂といった別当寺院を従えていた<ref>平凡社[1997: 109-110]</ref>。
高家の名の由来は、当王子が、中世の高家荘5ケ村の総氏神であったことによると考えられている<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/><ref>西[1987: 122]</ref>。異説によれば、現比定地の北が旧址であるといい、その根拠として『中右記』にある「高家荘司の仮屋」と現比定地との位置関係に矛盾があること、また現比定地の旧地名が王子脇であることが挙げられている<ref name="Heibon_1997_110">平凡社[1997: 110]</ref>。
例年10月16日・17日（[[1897年]]〈明治30年〉までは[[旧暦]]9月）に、近隣の萩原・荊木・池田・高家の4地域が集まって例祭「萩原祭」が執行される<ref name="Heibon_1997_110"/>。高家王子跡は和歌山県県指定史跡（1958年〈昭和33年〉4月1日指定）<ref>{{Cite web |publisher=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-10-04 }}</ref>。

本項目では、[[和歌山県]][[日高郡 (和歌山県)|日高郡]][[日高町 (和歌山県)|日高町]]に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。
== 九十九王子とは == {{Main|九十九王子}}
九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路|紀伊路・中辺路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から [[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。
しかしながら、[[1221年]]（[[承久]]3年）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。

== 日高町の九十九王子 == 日高町の九十九王子は4社。
=== 沓掛王子 === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|沓掛王子跡]] [[九十九王子 (広川町)#馬留王子|馬留王子]]（和歌山県[[有田郡]][[広川町 (和歌山県)|広川町]]）から南西に進み、急な坂道の[[鹿ヶ瀬峠]]（ししがせとうげ）の古道を越え、峠道を下りきったあたりを古い地名で王子谷といい、「熊野道間之愚記」（『[[明月記]]』所収）[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）10月10日条に「超此山参沓カケ王子」と記された'''沓掛王子'''（くつかけおうじ）の旧址である<ref name="Heibon_1997_234">平凡社[1997: 234]</ref>（日高町原谷902<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86">長谷川[2007: 86]</ref>）。
後世には鍵掛王子（かぎかけおうじ）と呼ばれたと見え、『[[紀伊続風土記]]』に「鍵掛王子 山口にあり、境内に弁財天社・長床あり」と記されていることを根拠に、前述の旧址とは別の字被喜の弁財天社（日高町原谷845<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86"/>）社地が社址と見なされてきた。しかし、「熊野道間之愚記」の記述と矛盾することや、王子谷に古くから小祠があって祭祀も行われていたことなどから<ref name="Heibon_1997_234"/>、時期は不明ながら弁財天社に移祀されたものと推定されている<ref name="Heibon_1997_234"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86"/>。
[[1877年]]（[[明治]]10年）、弁財天社とともに原谷皇太神社に合祀された<ref name="Heibon_1997_234"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_86"/>。字被喜の沓掛王子跡は日高町指定史跡（1973年〈昭和48年〉6月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|和書 |publisher=日高町教育委員会 |url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html |title=文化財・史跡明細 |accessdate=2009-10-04 }}</ref>。
* 所在地 - 日高町原谷845
=== 馬留王子 === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|馬留王子跡]] 沓掛王子から再び南西に進み、原谷皇太神社を過ぎて光明寺の境内に隣り合って'''馬留王子'''（うまどめおうじ）の石碑が立っている。しかし、『[[和歌山県聖蹟]]』は、実際の跡は石碑より上のミカン畑の中であるとしている<ref>西[1987: 120]</ref>。
『[[中右記]]』[[天仁]]2年（[[1109年]]）10月18日条に「馬留」の地名は見えるものの王子については触れられておらず、「熊野道間之愚記」（建仁元年〈1201年〉）にも記述は見えず、『紀伊続風土記』は「馬留王子社」について境内60[[間]]と記している<ref>平凡社[1997: 115]</ref>。現地では「間（ハザマ）王子」「駒留王子神社」とも称し、有田郡広川町の[[九十九王子 (広川町)#馬留王子|馬留王子]]と区別するために、広川町の馬留王子を東ノ馬留王子、日高町の馬留王子を西ノ馬留王子と称することがある<ref>長谷川[2007: 85-87]</ref>。
[[1907年]]（明治40年）、原谷皇太神社に合祀された<ref>長谷川[2007: 87]</ref>。日高町指定史跡（1973年〈昭和48年〉6月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
* 所在地 - 日高町原谷362
=== 内ノ畑王子 === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|内ノ畑王子跡]] 馬留王子から[[和歌山県道176号井関御坊線]]沿いに南に進み、西川を渡ったところにある今熊野神社の前の川岸が'''内ノ畑王子'''（うちのはたおうじ）の旧址である（日高町萩原1500<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89">長谷川[2007: 89]</ref>）。
『中右記』には見えず、「熊野道間之愚記」建仁元年（1201年）10月10日条に「内ノハタ王子」の名を記すほか、木の枝を刈って槌を作り、[[サカキ|榊]]の枝を結びつけて王子に持参する風習があったとしており、同様の記述が『熊野詣日記』（[[応永]]34年〈[[1427年]]〉）にも見られる<ref name="Heibon_1997_109">平凡社[1997: 109]</ref>。こうした風習により、当地では槌王子（つちおうじ）とも呼ばれた<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/><ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89"/>。
日高町には槌王子という字がある（日高町萩原1507<ref name="Hasegawa_2007_89"/>）が現在の社地とは離れていることから、もともと字槌王子にあった王子が近世までに衰退・荒廃し、現在の社地に移祀されたと推定されており、『紀伊続風土記』にもこの推定を裏付ける記述がある<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/>。[[1908年]]（明治41年）、原谷の今熊野神社に合祀され、その後内原王子神社に合祀された。今熊野王子社隣地の内ノ畑王子跡は日高町指定史跡（1973年〈昭和48年〉6月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen"/>。
*所在地 - 日高町萩原1500
=== 高家王子 === {{神社 |名称 = 内原王子神社 |画像 =[[File:高家王子.jpg|200px]] |所在地 = 和歌山県日高郡日高町萩原1670 |位置 = |祭神 = 皇太神 |別名 = 高家王子、東光寺王子 |例祭 = 10月16日・17日 }} 内ノ畑王子から西川沿いに南へ進み、内原王子神社の社地が'''高家王子'''（たいえおうじ）の旧址である。
『中右記』天仁2年（1109年）10月19日条に「大家王子」の名が見えるが、「熊野道間之愚記」（建仁元年〈1201年〉）には地名は見えるものの王子の記述はなく、『[[源平盛衰記]]』巻四〇に[[平維盛]]が参詣したとある<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/>。王子のある字王子脇に隣接して東光寺という地名が残ることから、近世には「若一王子社」「東光寺王子社」の名でも呼ばれ（『続紀伊風土記』）、法華堂・祈願堂・不動堂・毘沙門堂・護摩堂といった別当寺院を従えていた<ref>平凡社[1997: 109-110]</ref>。
高家の名の由来は、当王子が、中世の高家荘5ケ村の総氏神であったことによると考えられている<ref name="Heibon_1997_109"/><ref>西[1987: 122]</ref>。異説によれば、現比定地の北が旧址であるといい、その根拠として『中右記』にある「高家荘司の仮屋」と現比定地との位置関係に矛盾があること、また現比定地の旧地名が王子脇であることが挙げられている<ref name="Heibon_1997_110">平凡社[1997: 110]</ref>。
例年10月16日・17日（[[1897年]]〈明治30年〉までは[[旧暦]]9月）に、近隣の萩原・荊木・池田・高家の4地域が集まって例祭「萩原祭」が執行される<ref name="Heibon_1997_110"/>。高家王子跡は和歌山県県指定史跡（1958年〈昭和33年〉4月1日指定）<ref>{{Cite web|和書 |publisher=和歌山県教育委員会 |url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html |title=県指定文化財・記念物 |accessdate=2009-10-04 }}</ref>。
== 注 == {{Reflist|2}}
== 文献 == * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会編、1985、『和歌山県』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典30） ISBN 404001300X * 小山 靖憲、2000、『熊野古道』、岩波書店（岩波新書） ISBN 4004306655 * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社編、1997、『大和・紀伊寺院神社大事典』、平凡社 ISBN 458213402-5 * — 、1983、『和歌山県の地名』、平凡社（日本歴史地名大系31）

revision.comment: Adding an intro to all of them. It has a magic word instead of a substitution because all of them are gonna be moved in the future to proper pagenames. See comment for more info.
revision.text:
''{{PAGENAME}}'' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group '''{{PAGENAME}}''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 九十九王子 (日高町). <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview==

revision.comment: /* Overview */
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 日高町. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (日高町)-qq]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (日高町)-qq]] [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) located in **Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture**.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The **Kujūkuōji** (九十九王子) are a series of **shrines** situated along the **Kumano Kodō**, particularly the **Kiiji and Nakahechi routes**, that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the **12th to 13th centuries** to serve as **pilgrimage stops** for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to **praying for the protection** of the travelers.
However, after the **Jōkyū Rebellion** of **1221**, pilgrimages from **Kyoto** to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the **Kiiji Route** and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the **Muromachi period** onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the **Kishū Domain** carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the **Edo period**, they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the **Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto**, shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the **Kujūkuōji** whose locations are identified within **Hidaka Town**.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains **four Kujūkuōji shrines**.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the **Shishigase Pass** (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as **Ōji-dani** is the site of **Kutsukake Ōji** (沓掛王子), recorded in **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"** (*Meigetsuki*, **October 10, 1201**) as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as **Kagikake Ōji** (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a **Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)**, which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the **Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)**. However, this contradicts the **"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"**, and since a **small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries**, it is now believed that the shrine was **relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine** at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In **1877** (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine** along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The **Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki** was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**, a stone monument for **Umadome Ōji** (馬留王子) stands next to the **Kōmyōji temple grounds**. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the **actual site** is located **higher up in the mikan orchards**. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name **"Umadome"** appears in *Chūyūki* (**October 18, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an **Ōji Shrine**. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as **"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as **"Hazama Ōji"** or **"Komadome Ōji Shrine."** To distinguish it from **Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji**, that one is sometimes referred to as **"East Umadome Ōji"**, while the Hidaka Town one is called **"West Umadome Ōji."** {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In **1907** (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into **Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine**. It was designated as a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the **route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward**, crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of **Uchinohata Ōji** (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of **Imakumano Shrine**.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as **"Uchinohata Ōji,"** it notes that pilgrims **carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches** as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, **Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)**.
In **1908** (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into **Imakumano Shrine** in Haradani, and later **Uchihara Ōji Shrine**. The site was designated a **Hidaka Town Historic Site** in **1973** (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, **Uchihara Ōji Shrine** stands at the site of **Takaie Ōji** (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* (**October 19, 1109**, *Ten'nin 2*) as **"Ōie Ōji"**, it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where **Taira no Koremori** prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the **guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor**. It was also called **"Tōkōji Ōji"** and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an **annual festival on October 16-17**. It was designated a **Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site** in **1958** (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* **Location**: Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
