revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]==

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]==

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|九十九王子" to "|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]==

revision.comment: Text replacement - "九十九王子|" to "九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
==[[Kujūku Ōji template|List of the 99 Oji]]==

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--日高町)-->
<!--日高町)--> [[Category:last 150 pages as of feb 11]][[ja:九十九王子 (日高町)]] ==Overview== {{Refimprove|date=November 2023}} This article discusses the '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) located in '''Hidaka Town, Hidaka District, Wakayama Prefecture'''.
== What is Kujūkuōji? == {{Main|Kujūkuōji}}
The '''Kujūkuōji''' (九十九王子) are a series of '''shrines''' situated along the '''Kumano Kodō''', particularly the '''Kiiji and Nakahechi routes''', that were rapidly organized by the Kumano Shugendō guides during the '''12th to 13th centuries''' to serve as '''pilgrimage stops''' for the Imperial family and aristocrats on their Kumano pilgrimage. These shrines were dedicated to '''praying for the protection''' of the travelers.
However, after the '''Jōkyū Rebellion''' of '''1221''', pilgrimages from '''Kyoto''' to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the '''Kiiji Route''' and the gradual abandonment of many shrines. From the '''Muromachi period''' onward, as Kumano pilgrimage lost its former prominence, this trend accelerated. Although the '''Kishū Domain''' carried out efforts to restore the shrines during the '''Edo period''', they could not fully reverse the decline. Furthermore, with the '''Meiji era's nationalization of Shinto''', shrine mergers, and urbanization, many former shrine sites were lost or remain unidentified.
This article covers the '''Kujūkuōji''' whose locations are identified within '''Hidaka Town'''.
== Kujūkuōji in Hidaka Town == Hidaka Town contains '''four Kujūkuōji shrines'''.
=== Kutsukake Ōji === [[File:沓掛王子跡.jpg|thumb|Kutsukake Ōji site]] From [[Kujūkuōji (Hirokawa Town)#Umadome Ōji|Umadome Ōji]] (Hirokawa Town, Arida District, Wakayama Prefecture), the route continues southwest, crossing the '''Shishigase Pass''' (*Shishigase-tōge*). After descending the pass, the area historically known as '''Ōji-dani''' is the site of '''Kutsukake Ōji''' (沓掛王子), recorded in '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''' (*Meigetsuki*, '''October 10, 1201''') as *"Crossing this mountain, we visited Kutsukake Ōji."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}}.
In later periods, it was also known as '''Kagikake Ōji''' (鍵掛王子). The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* records that it contained a '''Benzaiten Shrine and a Nagatoko (long hall)''', which led to the belief that its shrine site was in a different location, at the '''Benzaiten Shrine in Hara Valley (Hidaka Town, Haradani 845)'''. However, this contradicts the '''"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"''', and since a '''small shrine had existed in Ōji-dani for centuries''', it is now believed that the shrine was '''relocated to the Benzaiten Shrine''' at some point {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}.
In '''1877''' (*Meiji 10*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''' along with the Benzaiten Shrine {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=234}} {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=86}}. The '''Kutsukake Ōji site in Aza Biki''' was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Hidaka Town Board of Education|url=http://www.town.hidaka.wakayama.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunka_meisai.html|title=List of Cultural Properties and Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 845
=== Umadome Ōji === [[File:馬留王子跡.jpg|thumb|Umadome Ōji site]] Proceeding southwest from Kutsukake Ōji past '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine''', a stone monument for '''Umadome Ōji''' (馬留王子) stands next to the '''Kōmyōji temple grounds'''. However, *Wakayama Prefecture Sacred Sites* suggests that the '''actual site''' is located '''higher up in the mikan orchards'''. {{Sfn|Nishi|1987|p=120}}
The name '''"Umadome"''' appears in *Chūyūki* ('''October 18, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*), though it does not explicitly mention an '''Ōji Shrine'''. It is also absent from *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki" (1201, Ken'nin 1)*. The *Kii Zoku Fudoki* describes it as '''"Umadome Ōji Shrine, covering an area of 60 ken (108 meters)."* {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=115}}. Locally, it is also known as '''"Hazama Ōji"''' or '''"Komadome Ōji Shrine."''' To distinguish it from '''Hirokawa Town's Umadome Ōji''', that one is sometimes referred to as '''"East Umadome Ōji"''', while the Hidaka Town one is called '''"West Umadome Ōji."''' {{Sfn|Hasegawa|2007|p=85-87}}.
In '''1907''' (*Meiji 40*), it was merged into '''Haradani Kōtaijin Shrine'''. It was designated as a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Haradani 362
=== Uchinohata Ōji === [[File:内ノ畑王子跡.jpg|thumb|Uchinohata Ōji site]] From Umadome Ōji, the '''route follows Wakayama Prefectural Route 176 southward''', crossing the Nishikawa River. The site of '''Uchinohata Ōji''' (内ノ畑王子) is located on the riverbank in front of '''Imakumano Shrine'''.
Recorded in *"Kumano Dō no Ma Gugoki"* (*October 10, 1201*) as '''"Uchinohata Ōji,"''' it notes that pilgrims '''carved wooden hammers and tied sakaki branches''' as offerings at the shrine, a practice also mentioned in *"Kumano Pilgrimage Diary" (1427, Ōei 34)*. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}. This tradition led to its alternate name, '''Tsuchi Ōji (Hammer Ōji)'''.
In '''1908''' (*Meiji 41*), it was merged into '''Imakumano Shrine''' in Haradani, and later '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine'''. The site was designated a '''Hidaka Town Historic Site''' in '''1973''' (*Shōwa 48*, June 9). <ref name="Kinen"/>
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1500
=== Takaie Ōji === [[File:高家王子.jpg|thumb|Takaie Ōji site (Uchihara Ōji Shrine)]] Following Nishikawa southward, '''Uchihara Ōji Shrine''' stands at the site of '''Takaie Ōji''' (高家王子).
Mentioned in *Chūyūki* ('''October 19, 1109''', *Ten'nin 2*) as '''"Ōie Ōji"''', it is also referenced in *Genpei Jōsuiki*, where '''Taira no Koremori''' prayed here. {{Sfn|Heibonsha|1997|p=109}}.
Takaie Ōji was historically the '''guardian deity of the five villages of Takaie Manor'''. It was also called '''"Tōkōji Ōji"''' and had affiliated temple halls. The shrine hosts an '''annual festival on October 16-17'''. It was designated a '''Wakayama Prefecture Historic Site''' in '''1958''' (*Shōwa 33*, April 1). <ref>{{Cite web|language=ja|publisher=Wakayama Prefectural Board of Education|url=http://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/mokuroku/kenkinen.html|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties - Historic Sites|accessdate=October 4, 2009}}</ref>.
* '''Location''': Hidaka Town, Hagiwara 1670
